description: Curated product documentation corpus for Noesis Health. Each chunk is a self-contained passage that answers a specific question about Noesis. Loaded into pgvector via tools/product_docs/load_product_docs.py with source_type='product_documentation'. The chat pipeline retrieves these alongside other RAG content, so questions like 'What does Noesis do?' get high-grounding answers sourced from authoritative product docs instead of LLM pretraining.

chunks.title: Noesis Health: product overview and positioning
chunks.text:
Noesis Health is a locally-deployed clinical intelligence platform for Clinical Documentation Integrity (CDI) teams. The one-sentence description: Noesis reconstructs the clinical story from a patient's record and highlights the diagnoses that materially change acuity — with the evidence needed to act on them.

The tagline is 'Clarity. Validation. Confidence.'

For a CDI specialist's daily workflow, the product statement is: 'When a CDI specialist opens a chart, Noesis scans the patient record and surfaces high-impact conditions that are supported clinically but not documented in the encounter — along with the evidence needed to act.'

Noesis is sold by Fox River AI (relufox.ai). It is currently in pre-pilot development with a target of hospital pilot engagements with mid-sized health systems (200-500 beds).

chunks.title: Who is Noesis Health for?
chunks.text:
Noesis Health serves five distinct buyer personas, each mapped to a different budget line:

1. CDI Specialists — automated specificity gap detection with a prioritized review queue, replacing manual hunting through charts.

2. Medical Coders — ICD-10 code suggestions backed by SNOMED CT evidence chains (source text → SNOMED concept → ICD-10 cross-map).

3. CDI Directors — program metrics, CMI trend, revenue opportunity dashboards, and boardroom-legible benchmarking against regional peers.

4. CFOs / HIM Directors — RAC audit defense via complete evidence trails, and CC/MCC capture tracking with dollar-value impact.

5. CMOs / Quality Committees — POA/HAC-aware flagging that routes safety-sensitive conditions to quality review instead of revenue opportunities, plus documentation completeness for malpractice defense ('if it isn't documented, it wasn't done').

The typical first customer is a mid-sized health system (200-500 beds) with CDI staffing pressure and revenue integrity concerns.

chunks.title: What does Noesis Health do? Core capabilities
chunks.text:
Noesis Health provides six core capabilities:

1. Clinical note analysis — concept extraction from unstructured clinical notes using a locally-running LLM (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B), with ontology-backed validation against SNOMED CT and RxNorm.

2. CDI specificity rules engine — 24 hand-curated rules covering the highest-impact CDI conditions (heart failure, sepsis, pneumonia, AKI, CKD, respiratory failure, encephalopathy, diabetes, malnutrition, anemia, pressure injury, COPD, atrial fibrillation, obesity, DVT, PE, hypertensive heart disease, stroke, coagulopathy, hyponatremia, hypokalemia, depression, alcohol dependence, opioid dependence). Each rule detects when documentation lacks the specificity needed for accurate ICD-10 assignment and generates a compliant CDI query with evidence.

3. DRG financial impact ranking — the Dashboard ranks documentation opportunities by estimated dollar impact using CMS MS-DRG v42 (FY2025) weights. This is how CDI Directors know which gaps to prioritize — the wedge.

4. CMS Hospital Compare peer benchmarking — real-time comparison of the hospital's Case-Mix Index against regional and national peers using free public CMS data. No other CDI tool leads with this.

5. Clinical Reference — a conversational chat interface backed by SNOMED CT tool-use and a curated clinical knowledge RAG, letting CDI specialists ask questions like 'What documentation does Noesis require for heart failure?' and get grounded answers.

6. Patient timeline — longitudinal FHIR resource assembly so CDI specialists can see the full clinical story.

Noesis is a documentation quality assurance tool, NOT clinical decision support. It flags documentation gaps and inconsistencies for physician review but does not make clinical recommendations, diagnoses, or treatment suggestions. This distinction is important for regulatory and liability reasons.

chunks.title: What Noesis does not do, limitations, out-of-scope features
chunks.text:
What does Noesis NOT do? What are Noesis's limitations? What is out of scope? What doesn't Noesis support?

Noesis Health is deliberately scoped to avoid features that would create regulatory risk, require proprietary dependencies, or dilute the product wedge:

- Noesis does NOT provide clinical decision support. It does not suggest treatments, diagnose patients, or offer medical advice. It is a documentation quality tool.

- Noesis does NOT use cloud-based LLMs. All inference runs on local hardware. No PHI ever leaves the hospital. No OpenAI, no Anthropic Claude, no Google Gemini in production paths.

- Noesis does NOT use APR-DRG (the All-Patient-Refined DRG system licensed commercially by Solventum/3M). Noesis v1 uses MS-DRG exclusively, which is free and CMS-published. APR-DRG may be added later via a complementary positioning model where Noesis feeds better documentation into the hospital's existing APR-DRG grouper.

- Noesis does NOT include a full MS-DRG grouper (v1). It uses a simpler CC/MCC impact model based on CMS MS-DRG triplet weight deltas. A full grouper (with MDC, age, sex, discharge status logic) is on the roadmap but explicitly out of scope for the first pilot.

- Noesis does NOT yet surface ICD-10-PCS specificity (procedure coding). v1 focuses on ICD-10-CM (diagnosis specificity). PCS is on the roadmap after CM capture is proven.

- Noesis does NOT process HL7 v2 messages. It consumes FHIR R4 resources, which the 21st Century Cures Act requires EHR systems to expose. HL7 v2 ingestion is an optional professional services add-on for hospitals with legacy systems.

- Noesis is NOT FDA-regulated as a medical device. It is a documentation quality assurance tool, not a clinical decision support system, and operates outside FDA jurisdiction for that reason.

chunks.title: Noesis Health three-service architecture
chunks.text:
Noesis Health is composed of three logical services that run on a two-machine on-premises deployment:

1. Noesis Gateway (port 8010): The FHIR resource consumer and analysis orchestrator. Handles Bulk FHIR / SMART on FHIR ingestion, Synthea synthetic data import, patient identity resolution, Dashboard aggregation queries, and orchestration of clinical analysis calls to Axiom Core. Written in Python (FastAPI + asyncpg). Repo: noesis-gateway.

2. Axiom Core (port 8008): The clinical intelligence pipeline. Runs concept extraction (via Ollama + Qwen3.5-35B-A3B LLM), SNOMED CT mapping, coverage checking, consistency validation, specificity rule evaluation, ICD-10 coding suggestions, and DRG impact calculation. Also hosts the /drg, /benchmark, and /rules APIs. Written in Python. Repo: genai-agent-core.

3. Noesis Clinical (port 1420): The React + Tauri desktop UI. Contains the CDI Dashboard, Patient Timeline, Coding Workbench, CDI Rules Browser, Clinical Reference chat panel, and Feedback Analytics admin view. Repo: genai-workshop.

All three services run on hospital-owned infrastructure. The two-machine deployment pattern is: Axiom Core + Gateway on a GPU workstation (2x NVIDIA A6000, 64-core Threadripper, 512GB RAM) running PostgreSQL with pgvector and Neo4j; Noesis Clinical runs on the user's workstation (Mac or Windows) and communicates with the backend over the facility LAN. No internet connectivity is required for inference or operation.

chunks.title: Axiom Core: the clinical intelligence pipeline
chunks.text:
Axiom Core is the backend clinical reasoning engine of the Noesis platform. It runs on the GPU workstation and provides these services:

- LLM inference via Ollama running Qwen3.5-35B-A3B (a Mixture-of-Experts model with 35B total parameters and 3B active per token, giving large-model quality at small-model latency). Model-per-GPU assignment on dual NVIDIA A6000s.

- SNOMED CT ontology: 382,170 concepts with 1.3 million relationships loaded into Neo4j (US 20250901 RF2 edition). Enables graph-based concept matching, IS_A traversal, and hierarchical specificity validation.

- RxNorm: 64,000+ drug concepts loaded for medication concept extraction.

- ICD-10-CM: 28,000 codes with 310,000 SNOMED → ICD-10 cross-map edges, enabling evidence chains from clinical text to billing code.

- pgvector: 445,000+ concept embeddings (768d, nomic-embed-text-v1.5) with HNSW index for fast semantic similarity search.

- CMS MS-DRG v42 weights (FY2025): 773 DRGs with 269 complete CC/MCC triplets loaded from the CMS IPPS Final Rule, enabling the CDI financial impact calculation.

- CMS Hospital Compare data: 5,426 US hospitals with 2,872 computed Case-Mix Index values for peer benchmarking.

- Specificity rules engine: 24 CDI rules in a three-layer architecture (Layer 1 = base rules that ship, Layer 2 = specialty rules reserved, Layer 3 = hospital-specific rules via UI).

- REST API: /validate/note, /drg/*, /benchmark/*, /rules/*, /ontology/snomed/*, /chat, /chat/stream, and more. Used by Noesis Gateway and by direct integrations.

Axiom Core is model-agnostic by design. The LLM can be swapped to DeepSeek-R1, Llama 3.3 70B, or Qwen 2.5 72B via configuration change only.

chunks.title: Noesis Gateway service
chunks.text:
What is Noesis Gateway? What does Noesis Gateway do? Noesis Gateway is one of the three services in the Noesis Health architecture. It is the FHIR resource consumer and analysis orchestrator — the facade that the React UI talks to. Noesis Gateway runs on port 8010 and is written in Python using FastAPI and asyncpg. The repo is 'noesis-gateway'.

Noesis Gateway handles three responsibilities:

1. FHIR R4 ingestion: Parses Bulk FHIR ndjson bundles, validates FHIR resources, resolves patient identity across encounters, and stores everything in the fhir_resources PostgreSQL table as JSONB. Currently supports Synthea synthetic data for development and testing; SMART on FHIR and Bulk FHIR production connectors are planned for Phase 2.

2. Analysis orchestration: When a clinical note arrives, Gateway enqueues it for analysis, calls Axiom Core's /validate/note endpoint, stores the resulting concepts, coverage gaps, specificity gaps, and ICD-10 suggestions in the clinical_analyses table, and marks the note ready for CDI review.

3. Dashboard + API aggregation: The UI calls Noesis Gateway (not Axiom Core directly) for Dashboard stats, patient lists, analysis queues, and benchmark data. Gateway runs the aggregation queries against the shared PostgreSQL instance and makes passthrough calls to Axiom Core for LLM-backed or ontology-backed operations.

Noesis Gateway exists as a separate service (rather than folding into Axiom Core or the UI) for two reasons: first, it enforces a clean separation between FHIR parsing concerns and clinical inference concerns. Second, it gives us a stable integration surface for future EHR-side connectors without touching the LLM pipeline.

Currently Noesis Gateway is tested with 116 Synthea files containing 153,000 FHIR resources across 113 synthetic patients — enough to drive realistic Dashboard demos. Bulk FHIR and SMART on FHIR connectors are the next ingestion priorities for Phase 2.

chunks.title: Noesis Clinical: the desktop UI
chunks.text:
Noesis Clinical is the React + Tauri desktop application that CDI specialists and coders actually interact with. It provides these tabs:

- Dashboard: The homepage. Shows CMS Peer Benchmark (your hospital vs state/national median CMI with dollar gap), Queue Status (pending/analyzing/complete), coverage distribution charts, analysis trends, and Documentation Opportunities ranked by dollar impact.

- Analysis Queue: Filterable list of analyzed notes (pending, approved, rejected, flagged), with searchable patient names, encounter dates, and inline analysis detail.

- Patient Timeline: Longitudinal view of a patient's FHIR resources (encounters, conditions, medications, observations, documents) with clinical dates extracted from each resource.

- Note Analysis: Paste or load a clinical note and run it through the full validation pipeline (concept extraction → SNOMED mapping → coverage check → consistency check → specificity rules → ICD-10 suggestions).

- Coding Workbench: Three-pane review interface (pending list, selected analysis detail, coding suggestions). CDI specialists approve, reject, or flag analyses here and eventually will draft and send CDI queries.

- CDI Rules: Browse all 24 specificity rules with their SNOMED codes, required attributes, ICD-10 mappings, and query templates. Includes an ontology lookup panel for writing or editing Layer 3 hospital rules.

- Clinical Reference: Conversational chat interface backed by SNOMED CT tool-use and a curated clinical knowledge RAG. Answers questions about CDI coding guidelines, clinical terminology, and Noesis itself.

- Feedback Analytics: Admin-only view showing thumbs-up/down ratings on LLM responses, with full prompt/response history and CSV export.

- About: Product overview and 58-term CDI glossary.

The UI is built with Tauri, which wraps the React app in a native desktop shell. This means no browser is required, PHI data stays off the network, and the app can access the facility's LAN directly to reach Gateway and Axiom Core.

chunks.title: Noesis CDI specificity rules engine
chunks.text:
Noesis ships with 24 CDI specificity rules in a three-layer architecture:

Layer 1 (Base rules) — Ship with the product and cannot be deleted. Cover the 24 highest-impact CDI conditions: heart failure (HF-001), sepsis (SEP-001), pneumonia (PNA-001), acute kidney injury (AKI-001), chronic kidney disease (CKD-001), respiratory failure (RF-001), encephalopathy (ENC-001), diabetes (DM-001), malnutrition (MAL-001), anemia (ANE-001), pressure injury (PUI-001), COPD (COPD-001), atrial fibrillation (AFB-001), obesity (OBE-001), deep vein thrombosis (DVT-001), pulmonary embolism (PE-001), hypertensive heart disease (HHD-001), cerebrovascular accident (STR-001), coagulopathy (COG-001), hyponatremia (LYTE-001), hypokalemia (LYTE-002), major depressive disorder (DEP-001), alcohol dependence (ALC-001), and opioid dependence (OPI-001).

Layer 2 (Specialty rules) — Reserved for future condition libraries by specialty (cardiology, oncology, nephrology, etc.). Not yet populated.

Layer 3 (Hospital rules) — Facility-specific rules created via the UI. Hospital CDI Directors can add rules for query patterns unique to their patient population, override base rules (e.g., lower the priority of atrial fibrillation for a population where it's already well-documented), or add new conditions entirely. This is how the product stays flexible without vendor involvement.

Each rule has: a condition name, triggering SNOMED codes, unspecified ICD-10 code(s), a map of specific ICD-10 code variants, required documentation attributes (with evidence patterns for the LLM), a CDI query template, a priority level (high/medium/low), and a layer marker. Rules are evaluated after concept extraction and SNOMED mapping, and each rule violation becomes a specificity_gap in the analysis output with evidence linking source text → SNOMED concept → ICD-10 mapping.

The negation checker is a two-layer defensive system: the LLM's extraction prompt explicitly instructs it to mark negated concepts, and a regex-based scan runs over the source text around each matched concept to catch any negation the LLM missed.

chunks.title: Noesis DRG financial impact calculator
chunks.text:
Noesis ranks Documentation Opportunities on the Dashboard by estimated revenue impact in dollars — not by count. This is the core of the product wedge: 'Noesis finds and ranks the highest-impact comorbidities missing from the documentation.'

The calculator is scoped as a CC/MCC impact model using CMS MS-DRG v42 (FY2025) data. It is NOT a full MS-DRG grouper (age, sex, MDC, and discharge status logic are out of scope for v1) and it does NOT use the licensed APR-DRG system from Solventum/3M.

How it works: For each specificity gap, the calculator operates in one of two modes depending on the rule type:

1. Principal diagnosis mode — For conditions like heart failure, pneumonia, sepsis, AKI, stroke, PE, DVT, and respiratory failure that have their own named DRG triplet in the CMS MS-DRG definitions. The calculator looks up the current DRG (typically the 'without CC/MCC' variant), the target DRG (typically the 'with MCC' variant), computes the delta weight, multiplies by the CMS national base rate ($7,200 for FY2025), and returns the exact per-case impact. Example: HF-001 unspecified (DRG 293, weight 0.5490) → acute systolic heart failure (DRG 291, weight 1.3049) = 0.7559 weight delta × $7,200 = $5,442 per case.

2. Comorbidity mode — For conditions like malnutrition, encephalopathy, anemia, and coagulopathy that are secondary diagnoses rather than principal diagnoses. These shift the DRG tier of whatever principal diagnosis the patient has, rather than having their own DRG triplet. The calculator uses empirical averages from CMS data: NONE→CC = 0.5038 weight delta (~$3,627), CC→MCC = 1.3188 weight delta (~$9,495), NONE→MCC = 1.8111 weight delta (~$13,040). These numbers are empirical averages computed across all 269 complete CC/MCC triplets in FY2025.

Of the 24 base rules, 19 return real dollar values. 5 legitimately return None: obesity (requires BMI cross-coding), pressure injury (rule content needs stage 3/4 differentiation), respiratory failure (rule content issue), hyponatremia and hypokalemia (rule content placeholders), and opioid dependence (no CC/MCC improvement available). These are rule content issues, not calculator bugs, and are tracked as backlog items.

Total opportunity across a hospital's notes is computed as: sum(per-condition impact × count of notes affected), sorted descending. This is what the Dashboard's Documentation Opportunities table displays.

chunks.title: CMS Hospital Compare peer benchmarking, CMI, non-IPPS hospital handling
chunks.text:
How does Noesis handle Boston Children's Hospital? How does Noesis handle MD Anderson? How does Noesis handle critical access hospitals? Why doesn't this hospital have a CMI? What is the CMS Hospital Compare benchmark?

Noesis leads the Dashboard with a real-time CMS peer benchmark — a feature no commercial CDI tool promotes and one of the product's strongest differentiators.

Every commercial CDI tool (Iodine, Solventum 360 Encompass, Nuance, Streamline) shows hospitals their own numbers in isolation. Noesis compares the hospital against its peers using publicly-available CMS data, updated quarterly.

What's loaded: Noesis has 5,426 US hospitals with demographics (from CMS Hospital General Information), 2,990 Medicare inpatient provider aggregates with chronic condition percentages and risk scores, 145,909 hospital × DRG discharge rows, and 2,872 computed Case-Mix Index values. CMI is calculated as the weighted average of MS-DRG relative weights across each hospital's 2023 Medicare discharge mix: CMI = SUM(drg_weight × discharges) / SUM(discharges). Verified against public reporting: Northwestern Memorial 2.7489, Mass General 2.7126, Mayo Rochester 2.6761, national median 1.6052.

How it works for a CDI Director: the Dashboard's Benchmark panel shows (a) the hospital's current CMI, (b) its national percentile, (c) its state percentile, and (d) the revenue gap to state median in dollars (CMI delta × annual discharges × base rate). The panel also shows a collapsible 'Peer Hospitals in [city]' drill-down listing every other hospital in the same city, sorted by CMI. Clicking any peer switches the Dashboard to that hospital's benchmark.

Special handling for non-IPPS facilities: Noesis classifies every US hospital into one of 9 facility types (IPPS, CAH, PSYCH, PEDS, VA, DoD, REH, LTCH, PCH) and renders a type-specific explanation when CMI doesn't apply. For example, selecting Boston Children's Hospital shows a panel explaining that children's hospitals have minimal Medicare volume so CMS doesn't publish MS-DRG CMI for them, and MD Anderson shows a panel explaining that PPS-Exempt Cancer Hospitals are paid under a separate reimbursement system. This turns data gaps into credibility moments.

Hospital name aliasing: the product ships with 28 curated aliases (MD Anderson, MSK, Dana-Farber, Cleveland Clinic, Hopkins, Mayo, Cedars-Sinai, Northwestern, UChicago, Duke, Penn HUP, UPMC, Stanford, UCSF, UCLA, Vanderbilt, Brigham, Mass General, Moffitt, Roswell Park, Fox Chase, Fred Hutch, Boston Childrens, CHOP, CHLA, Texas Childrens, Seattle Childrens, St Jude, Nationwide, Lurie, Lucile Packard, Children's Dallas) so users can search for brand names that differ from the CMS legal names.

chunks.title: Noesis Clinical Reference: RAG chat with tool use
chunks.text:
The Clinical Reference tab is a conversational chat interface that lets CDI specialists ask questions about CDI coding, clinical terminology, Noesis rules, and SNOMED CT concepts in natural language. It's backed by:

- Qwen3.5-35B-A3B LLM running locally via Ollama (no cloud)
- RAG retrieval from pgvector with 445,000+ embedded concepts and documentation chunks
- Tool-use via OpenAI-compatible function calling, with 8 tools that let the LLM query SNOMED CT (search, detail, descendants, ancestors) and the CDI rules engine (list, detail, find_by_condition, find_by_snomed) directly from the database
- Conversation history with multi-turn context preservation
- Grounding score (0-1) showing how much of the answer is backed by retrieved context vs LLM pretraining
- Feedback capture (thumbs-up/down) linked to individual LLM calls for quality tracking

Example questions that work well: 'What documentation does Noesis require for heart failure?', 'What SNOMED concepts are descendants of sepsis?', 'What's the CMI for Northwestern Memorial?', 'How does the DRG grouper work?', 'What's the difference between sepsis and severe sepsis per Sepsis-3?'

The panel displays a low-grounding warning banner (under 60%) when the response is drawn primarily from the LLM's general knowledge rather than Noesis's curated data. This is a deliberate honesty feature — CDI specialists should know when to verify against official guidelines (CMS MS-DRG, ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines, ACDIS, Sepsis-3).

Clinical Reference is NOT a clinical decision support tool. It answers questions about documentation, coding, and terminology. It does not suggest treatments, diagnoses, or clinical actions.

chunks.title: Noesis local inference, cloud usage, privacy, and deployment model
chunks.text:
Is Noesis cloud-based? Does Noesis use OpenAI? Where is Noesis hosted? Does Noesis send PHI to the cloud?

Noesis Health runs entirely on hospital-owned infrastructure. It is NOT cloud-based. It does NOT use OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, or Google Gemini for production inference. PHI never leaves the hospital. This is a strategic differentiator, not just a technical choice.

Why local inference matters:

1. No PHI egress — Patient data never leaves the hospital. No cloud LLM provider, no external API, no third-party data sharing. This eliminates a category of HIPAA and Business Associate Agreement risk that cloud-based CDI tools have to mitigate.

2. No dependency on vendor cloud uptime — Noesis works even if the hospital loses internet connectivity, as long as the facility LAN is up. Cloud CDI tools are dead when their vendor's cloud has an outage.

3. Predictable cost model — Hospitals buy the hardware once (approximately $15-30k for a usable GPU workstation) and the operating cost is just electricity. Cloud-based CDI tools bill per-chart-analyzed or per-user, which can become very expensive at scale.

4. Data sovereignty — For academic medical centers with research IRBs and for hospitals in states with strict data localization requirements, local inference is a procurement-level requirement, not a preference.

5. Model swappability — Because inference runs on local GPUs via Ollama, Noesis is model-agnostic. The LLM can be swapped to DeepSeek-R1, Llama 3.3 70B, Qwen 2.5 72B, or any future open-weight model via a config change. No retraining, no vendor lock-in.

6. Audit defense — Every LLM call is logged to PostgreSQL with prompt, response, grounding score, and tool calls. This creates an audit trail that's available for RAC defense or internal compliance review without relying on a vendor to produce it.

The hardware profile for a production deployment is: AMD Threadripper or equivalent 64-core CPU, 512GB DDR5 RAM, 2x NVIDIA A6000 GPUs (48GB VRAM each, NVLink connected), fast NVMe storage for PostgreSQL and Neo4j. This fits in a single 4U rack unit and draws about 1.5kW. A smaller deployment for a community hospital can use a single A6000 or RTX 6000 Ada.

chunks.title: Noesis Health current phase status
chunks.text:
Noesis Health is in pre-pilot development. Current status by phase:

Phase 1A (FHIR Ingestion): COMPLETE. 116 Synthea synthetic patient files containing 153,000 FHIR resources across 113 patients have been loaded and are used for demos and integration testing.

Phase 1B (Clinical Intelligence Pipeline): COMPLETE. Gateway ↔ Axiom Core integration is working end-to-end. Clinical notes flow from ingestion through concept extraction, SNOMED mapping, coverage checking, consistency validation, specificity rules, ICD-10 suggestion, and DRG impact calculation without manual intervention.

Phase 1C (CDI User Interface): COMPLETE. Dashboard, Analysis Queue, Patient Timeline, Note Analysis, Coding Workbench, CDI Rules Browser, Clinical Reference chat, Feedback Analytics, and About pages are all built and deployed.

Phase 1D (Demo & Validation): IN PROGRESS. The CMS Hospital Compare benchmarking feature is live. The DRG grouper with real CC/MCC financial impact is live. 19 of 24 CDI rules are returning real dollar values. Known gaps: CMI projected card (not yet built), POA/HAC safety routing (designed but not built), more complete clinical RAG library (Sepsis-3, CMS guidelines partially loaded).

Phase 2 (Bulk FHIR + SMART on FHIR production connectors): NOT STARTED. Required for first real hospital pilot.

Agent architecture phases P1-P3 have shipped in parallel with the above: observability (Phase 1 — llm_calls logging, feedback capture, eval harness), SNOMED MCP tool-use (Phase 2), CDI Rules MCP tool-use (Phase 3), and feedback analytics admin dashboard (Phase 4b). Phases 4 (Clinical RAG library build-out), 5 (Case Reviewer agent — the 'moat feature'), and 6+ (differentiator agents for Consistency, POA Timeline, and Coding Audit) are on the roadmap.

The first hospital pilot is the next major milestone. Noesis is being presented to MIT advisor Dr. Bruce Lam in April 2026 with the goal of initiating CDI specialist introductions.
